title: Tests keep failing in CI but pass on my machine
body: Our CI pipeline has been randomly failing for the past few days. The test suite passes fine when I run it locally. The failures seem random — sometimes all tests pass, sometimes a handful fail. It's blocking our releases and we can't figure out what's going on.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
Thanks for reporting this — flaky CI is painful, especially when it blocks releases.

To start narrowing this down: when the tests fail in CI, what do the failure messages actually say? Are they assertion errors, timeouts, connection refused, segfaults — what exactly? And can you share a link to (or paste the relevant output from) a recent failed CI run?

Also: did anything change around the time this started a few days ago — a dependency update, a CI config change, a new test added?

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: They're assertion errors. The test names are right there in the logs — it's the new ordering tests that got added last week. Look, they pass on my machine every single time. A coworker merged a PR with some new task ordering tests like 4 days ago and that's when this started. Can someone just fix this? It's blocking us.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
I hear you — flaky CI blocking releases is urgent, and we'll get this sorted.

The new ordering tests are the prime suspect since the timing lines up perfectly. To zero in on the fix: can you paste the actual assertion output from one of the failures? I'm looking for something like `expected [A, B, C] but got [B, A, C]` — the specific expected-vs-actual values will tell us whether these tests are assuming a deterministic order that the CI environment doesn't guarantee (e.g., database query order, timestamp-based sorting with ties).

Also — do the tests in CI run in parallel, or does CI use a different database/configuration than your local setup?

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: I don't remember the exact output off the top of my head but yeah it's basically that — expected one order, got a different order. The items are the same just shuffled around. And yes we use a different config in CI vs local, it's supposed to be more production-like. I don't know all the details of what's different. Can you just look at the PR that added those tests? It was merged about 4 days ago. The tests are clearly the problem.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
I think I have a good read on this. My working theory: the new ordering tests are asserting a specific sequence of results, but the underlying query or sorting logic doesn't fully guarantee that order — for example, sorting by a timestamp where multiple tasks share the same value, or relying on database insertion order instead of an explicit `ORDER BY`. Your local environment happens to return a consistent order, but the CI database (different engine or config) doesn't.

To get a developer straight to the fix: **can you share the PR number or link for the PR your coworker merged?** That way someone can look at the test assertions directly and confirm whether they need to add a tiebreaker to the sort or relax the assertion to check set-equality instead of sequence-equality.

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: I don't have the PR number handy, I'd have to go dig it up. It was my coworker's PR, not mine. It's the most recent merge into main from about 4 days ago — it shouldn't be hard to find. It added like 5 new tests to the task ordering module. Can someone on your end just look at the recent merge history? I've already spent too much time on this.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body: [RESOLVED] {"title":"Flaky CI: New task ordering tests assert deterministic sequence on non-deterministic query results","problem":"Five new tests added to the task ordering module ~4 days ago are intermittently failing in CI with assertion errors. The tests expect items in a specific sequence, but receive the same items in a different order. Tests pass consistently in local development but fail randomly in CI, which uses a different, more production-like database configuration.","root_cause_hypothesis":"The new ordering tests assert exact sequence equality on query results that lack a fully deterministic sort. The underlying query likely sorts by a field (e.g., timestamp, priority) where ties are possible, without a tiebreaker column (e.g., `id`). The local database engine or config happens to return a stable insertion-order for tied rows, while the CI database does not, exposing the non-determinism.","reproduction_steps":["Check out main at the commit where the ordering tests were added (~4 days ago)","Run the task ordering test suite against the CI database configuration","Observe intermittent assertion failures where expected and actual arrays contain the same items in different order"],"environment":"CI environment with a 'more production-like' database configuration (exact differences unknown to the reporter). Local development environment uses a different config that masks the non-determinism.","severity":"high","impact":"Blocks all releases. The entire team is affected — CI failures are intermittent so retrying sometimes works, but the pipeline is unreliable.","recommended_fix":"1. Find the PR merged ~4 days ago that added ~5 tests to the task ordering module (most recent merge to main around that date). 2. Inspect the test assertions: replace exact sequence assertions (`assertEqual([A,B,C], result)`) with either (a) set/bag equality where order doesn't matter, or (b) add a deterministic tiebreaker (e.g., `ORDER BY priority, id`) to the underlying query so the order is guaranteed. 3. Audit the application query itself — if the UI or API depends on this ordering, the tiebreaker should be added to the production query, not just the tests.","proposed_test_case":"Create a test scenario where multiple tasks share the same sort-key value (e.g., same priority or same timestamp), then assert that the query returns them in a fully deterministic order (by tiebreaker). Run this test 20+ times in a loop to confirm stability.","information_gaps":["Exact PR number (findable from merge history — no reporter action needed)","Specific CI database engine and config differences vs local (inspectable from CI config files)","Whether the non-deterministic order also affects production behavior or only tests"]}
